topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the entryway table like a silent, brightly colored accusation. I stared at it, my hands trembling so violently I had to grip the edge of the mahogany. Petals of impossible crimson and electric yellow spilled out, a chaotic, fragrant explosion against the polished wood. There was no note. Just the flowers. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow thing in my chest. I circled the table twice, my gaze darting from bloom to bloom, searching desperately for a clue, a whisper of intent. My palms began to sweat, the dampness seeping into my skin. Why? Who would do this? A sudden, dizzying wave washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I backed away slowly, bumping into the coat rack, and let out a small, strangled sound that felt entirely too loud in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

I picked up the bouquet as if it were radioactive. The stems were cool and slick beneath my fingertips, the scent—a heady mix of gardenia and something sharp, like ozone—flooding my senses. I turned the arrangement over, expecting a hidden compartment, a tiny slip of paper tucked beneath the wrapping. Nothing. Just the vibrant, unapologetic display. My vision seemed to narrow, the edges of the room blurring into streaks of color. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo demanding attention. I paced the length of the living room, my steps quick and uneven, my jaw tight. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened windowpane; my face was pale, my eyes wide and unfocused, betraying a profound internal tremor. I dropped the flowers onto the rug with a muffled thud, unable to look at them again.

[story 3]

It was just there, resting on the counter next to my lukewarm coffee: a lavish arrangement of lilies and deep purple roses. No inscription. No signature. The silence of the morning amplified the frantic rhythm inside my own skull. I picked one up, the velvet texture of the petals feeling alien and too heavy in my hand. My fingers tightened around the stem until the wood creaked faintly. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a hot, stinging sensation that made me blink rapidly, trying to clear the sudden visual static. I stood frozen by the kitchen island, swaying slightly. Every nerve ending felt exposed, screaming in a high-pitched frequency. I let out a short, sharp laugh that caught in my throat, a sound devoid of humor, and then I simply couldn't stand still. I began to pace in tight, jerky circles, my gaze fixed on the flowers as if they held the entire answer to a question I couldn't possibly comprehend.
